---
title: "Exercise: Parsing BED by hand"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Write `parse_bed_line(line: &str) -> Result<BedRecord, BedError>`: parse one line of a [BED file](https://samtools.github.io/hts-specs/BEDv1.pdf) (chromosome, start, end — the three mandatory fields) into a struct. Then use it from `main` to parse a whole file.

This is the day's warm-up: we get a real bioinformatics format flowing through Rust **using only the standard library** before we reach for external crates. On day 4 exercise 2 we will let [`noodles`](https://docs.rs/noodles/) do the same thing for FASTA, and you will see what the crate is buying us.

## Background

BED is tab-separated text, one interval per line. The first three columns are always:

```
chrom   start   end
```

`start` and `end` are half-open zero-based coordinates, as on [day 3 exercise 1](../day3/01-genomic-interval.qmd). Many real BED files have more columns (name, score, strand, ...); we will ignore them today and just take the first three.

## The exercise

```bash
cd day4/ex-bed-parse
```

Implement `parse_bed_line`. Make all six unit tests pass. The starter also includes a `main` that reads a file path from the CLI and prints a summary; once your function works, run it on the bundled `data/sample.bed`.

## How to run

```bash
cargo test
cargo run -- data/sample.bed   # summary of intervals
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — splitting on tabs"}
[`str::split`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.str.html#method.split) takes a delimiter and returns an iterator. Collect the first three pieces:

```rust
let mut parts = line.split('\t');
let chrom = parts.next().ok_or(BedError::TooFewFields)?;
let start_s = parts.next().ok_or(BedError::TooFewFields)?;
let end_s = parts.next().ok_or(BedError::TooFewFields)?;
```

Any extra columns are simply ignored — call `parts.next()` only as many times as you need.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — parsing the numbers"}
Same pattern as day 3's `parse_region`: use [`str::parse`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.str.html#method.parse) and [`Result::map_err`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/result/enum.Result.html#method.map_err) to convert the parse error into our domain enum:

```rust
let start: u64 = start_s.parse().map_err(|_| BedError::InvalidStart)?;
let end:   u64 = end_s  .parse().map_err(|_| BedError::InvalidEnd)?;
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — the order check"}
BED allows `end == start` (a zero-length interval, sometimes used as an insertion point), but `end < start` is malformed:

```rust
if end < start {
    return Err(BedError::EndBeforeStart);
}
Ok(BedRecord { chrom: chrom.to_string(), start, end })
```
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```rust
fn parse_bed_line(line: &str) -> Result<BedRecord, BedError> {
    let mut parts = line.split('\t');
    let chrom = parts.next().ok_or(BedError::TooFewFields)?;
    let start_s = parts.next().ok_or(BedError::TooFewFields)?;
    let end_s = parts.next().ok_or(BedError::TooFewFields)?;
    let start: u64 = start_s.parse().map_err(|_| BedError::InvalidStart)?;
    let end:   u64 = end_s  .parse().map_err(|_| BedError::InvalidEnd)?;
    if end < start {
        return Err(BedError::EndBeforeStart);
    }
    Ok(BedRecord { chrom: chrom.to_string(), start, end })
}
```

Full reference in `day4/ex-bed-parse-solution/src/main.rs`.
:::

## Where this leads

- **Exercise 02**: same pattern, but for FASTA, with [`noodles-fasta`](https://docs.rs/noodles-fasta/) handling the parsing. Notice how much less code is yours and how much comes from the crate.
- A real BED parser would also accept `noodles-bed`, support BED12 (12-column variant), and parse strand. We leave those as natural extensions.
